title: Failure Modes & Resilience
summary: Split brain, cascading failures, bulkhead pattern, circuit breaker, retry strategies, and designing for failure.

sections.content:
## Expect Failure

In distributed systems, failure is not an exception — it's the norm. Hardware fails, networks partition, services time out. Resilient systems are designed assuming failure will happen.

**Key failures to design for:**
- Node crashes
- Network partitions (split brain)
- Slow (not failed) dependencies
- Cascading failures
- Memory/CPU pressure

## Split Brain

Split brain occurs when a cluster loses network connectivity and **each partition believes it is the primary** — both accept writes, leading to data divergence.

```
Normal: Leader ←──────── Follower
Split:  Leader ✗✗✗✗✗✗✗✗ Follower
        Both think they're leader!
        Both accept writes!
        Data diverges!
```

**Prevention:**
- **Quorum-based systems** (Raft, ZooKeeper) — only the majority partition can elect a leader
- **STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head)** — forcibly kill the node in the minority partition
- **Fencing tokens** — monotonically increasing tokens; old leader's writes are rejected by storage

sections.title: Split Brain and Quorum Prevention
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Split Brain - No Quorum
        P1[Partition 1\nNode 1 + 2] 
        P2[Partition 2\nNode 3 + 4 + 5]
        P1 -->|I am leader!| W1[Accept writes]
        P2 -->|I am leader!| W2[Accept writes]
        W1 & W2 --> Conflict[DATA CONFLICT!]
    end
    subgraph Quorum Prevention N=5
        QP1[2 nodes\nminority] -->|cannot reach quorum| X[REFUSE writes]
        QP2[3 nodes\nmajority] -->|has quorum| OK[Accept writes SAFELY]
    end
    style Conflict fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style X fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style OK fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Cascading Failures

A cascading failure occurs when one service's failure causes dependent services to fail, spreading across the system.

```
Service C is slow (DB under load)
→ Service B's threads block waiting for C
→ Service B's thread pool exhausts
→ Service A can't get responses from B
→ Service A's threads block
→ Entire system down
```

### Prevention strategies:
- **Timeouts** — never wait indefinitely; fail fast
- **Circuit Breaker** — stop calling a failed service
- **Bulkhead** — isolate failures to a pool
- **Backpressure** — slow down when overwhelmed

sections.title: Circuit Breaker State Machine
sections.content:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed: Start
    Closed --> Open: Error threshold exceeded
    Open --> HalfOpen: Timeout expires (try again)
    HalfOpen --> Closed: Test request succeeds
    HalfOpen --> Open: Test request fails
    Closed: CLOSED\nNormal operation\nRequests pass through
    Open: OPEN\nFast fail\nReturn fallback immediately
    HalfOpen: HALF-OPEN\nAllow 1 test request

rows.How: Fail after N ms, don't wait forever
Fast-fail when error rate is high

sections.question: What is the main purpose of adding jitter to retry delays?
options: To make retries faster on average
To prevent all clients from retrying simultaneously (retry storm) after an outage
To comply with rate limiting requirements
To reduce memory usage during retries
sections.explanation: Without jitter, all clients that failed at the same time will retry at the same time (e.g. all retry after exactly 100ms). This creates a synchronized retry storm that can overwhelm the recovering service. Jitter adds randomness to spread retries over time, giving the service a chance to recover gradually.
